1 April 1973; 52 years ago (1 April 1973) (as *Pohang Iron & Steel Company FC*)

([Hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul"): 포항 스틸러스) are a South Korean professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Pohang](/wiki/Pohang "Pohang"), [North Gyeongsang Province](/wiki/North_Gyeongsang_Province "North Gyeongsang Province") that compete in the [K League 1](/wiki/K_League_1 "K League 1"), the top flight of [South Korean football](/wiki/Football_in_South_Korea "Football in South Korea"). The club was founded on 1 April 1973 as Pohang Iron & Steel Company FC, named after the steel company [POSCO](/wiki/POSCO "POSCO"), which still owns the club today.[[1]](#cite_note-afc50-1) They are one of South Korea's most successful teams, having won the K League five times and the [AFC Champions League](/wiki/AFC_Champions_League "AFC Champions League") three times.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The **Pohang Steelers** ([Hangul](/wiki/Hangul "Hangul"): 포항 스틸러스) are a South Korean professional [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") club based in [Pohang](/wiki/Pohang "Pohang"), [North Gyeongsang Province](/wiki/North_Gyeongsang_Province "North Gyeongsang Province") that compete in the [K League 1](/wiki/K_League_1 "K League 1"), the top flight of [South Korean football](/wiki/Football_in_South_Korea "Football in South Korea"). The club was founded on 1 April 1973 as Pohang Iron & Steel Company FC, named after the steel company [POSCO](/wiki/POSCO "POSCO"), which still owns the club today.[[1]](#cite_note-afc50-1) They are one of South Korea's most successful teams, having won the K League five times and the [AFC Champions League](/wiki/AFC_Champions_League "AFC Champions League") three times.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pohang_Steelers&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The club was founded on 1 April 1973 as Pohang Iron & Steel Company FC.[[1]](#cite_note-afc50-1)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) Upon its establishment, the team consisted of 13 players, including South Korea international [Lee Hoe-taik](/wiki/Lee_Hoe-taik "Lee Hoe-taik"), and was led by manager [Han Hong-ki](/wiki/Han_Hong-ki "Han Hong-ki").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) The following year, Pohang Steel won their first trophy, beating [Sungkyunkwan University](/wiki/Sungkyunkwan_University "Sungkyunkwan University") 2–1 in the final of the [President's Cup](/wiki/Korean_President%27s_Cup_National_Football_Tournament "Korean President's Cup National Football Tournament").[[5]](#cite_note-5) The club also joined the [Korean National Semi-professional Football League](/wiki/Korean_National_Semi-Professional_Football_League "Korean National Semi-Professional Football League") and won their first title in the spring league of 1975.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Their second title came in the autumn league of 1981, and when the competition was re-organised as an all-year league in 1982, Pohang lifted the trophy once again.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Renamed as the Pohang Iron & Steel Dolphins, the club was one of the founding members of the [Korean Super League](/wiki/K_League "K League") in 1983.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The same year, Pohang became the first team in the league to field foreign players, signing midfielder [Sergio](/wiki/Sergio_(footballer,_born_1960) "Sergio (footballer, born 1960)") and forward [Jose](/wiki/Jose_Roberto_Alves "Jose Roberto Alves"), who had previously been playing for the football team of a Brazilian steelmaking company.[[10]](#cite_note-10) Initially competing as a [semi-professional](/wiki/Semi-professional "Semi-professional") club, the Dolphins turned professional in the 1984 season, and rebranded again a year later, this time as the Pohang Iron & Steel Atoms.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

In [1986](/wiki/1986_K_League "1986 K League") they won their first championship, and enjoyed a spell of domination in the league; between 1985 and 1998 they were continuously in the top four of the [K League](/wiki/K_League "K League"). In 1995, the club was renamed again, becoming the Pohang Atoms. This name change was an attempt to further strengthen local ties with the region, and in 1997 they adopted their current name, the Pohang Steelers. The team won the [Asian Champions Cup](/wiki/AFC_Champions_League "AFC Champions League") in [1997](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Asian_Club_Championship "1996–97 Asian Club Championship") and [1998](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Asian_Club_Championship "1997–98 Asian Club Championship").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the 2000s, the club struggled near the bottom of the table, but bounced back to the forefront of South Korean football by winning the first stage of the [2004 K League Championship](/wiki/2004_K_League_Championship "2004 K League Championship"). The club qualified for the final Championship match of the [2004 season](/wiki/2004_K_League "2004 K League"), but lost 4–3 on [penalties](/wiki/Penalty_shoot-out_(association_football) "Penalty shoot-out (association football)") to [Suwon Samsung Bluewings](/wiki/Suwon_Samsung_Bluewings "Suwon Samsung Bluewings").[[14]](#cite_note-14)

In 2007, the club won the [Championship play-off](/wiki/2007_K_League_Championship "2007 K League Championship") by beating [Seongnam Ilhwa Chunma](/wiki/Seongnam_FC "Seongnam FC"), who finished in first place in the [regular season](/wiki/2007_K_League "2007 K League") of the K League. Pohang won the first leg 3–1 at home, and then traveled to Seongnam for the second leg game, recording a 1–0 victory to seal a 4–1 aggregate triumph. The Steelers had ended the K League season in fifth place, but then defeated [Gyeongnam FC](/wiki/Gyeongnam_FC "Gyeongnam FC"), [Ulsan Hyundai Horang-i](/wiki/Ulsan_Hyundai_FC "Ulsan Hyundai FC"), Suwon Samsung Bluewings and finally Seongnam Ilhwa Chunma in the play-offs to win the championship.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Pohang again made the play-offs in the [2008 season](/wiki/2008_K_League "2008 K League") by finishing the season in fifth place, but were knocked out in their play-off game by Ulsan Hyundai after the penalty shoot-out. However, the club fared much better in the [2008 Korean FA Cup](/wiki/2008_Korean_FA_Cup "2008 Korean FA Cup"). After beating Seongnam Ilhwa Chunma in the quarter-finals, Pohang knocked out [Daegu FC](/wiki/Daegu_FC "Daegu FC") in the semi-finals and then defeated Gyeongnam FC in the final to ensure qualification for the [2009 AFC Champions League](/wiki/2009_AFC_Champions_League "2009 AFC Champions League").[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In the 2009 AFC Champions League, the Steelers defeated [Umm-Salal](/wiki/Umm_Salal_SC "Umm Salal SC") of Qatar 4–1 on aggregate in the semi-finals to advance to their [first ever AFC Champions League final](/wiki/2009_AFC_Champions_League_Final "2009 AFC Champions League Final").[[17]](#cite_note-17) The Steelers defeated Saudi club [Al-Ittihad](/wiki/Ittihad_FC "Ittihad FC") 2–1 at the [National Stadium](/wiki/National_Olympic_Stadium_(Tokyo) "National Olympic Stadium (Tokyo)") in [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), Japan to claim the title.[[18]](#cite_note-18)[[19]](#cite_note-19) For the [2009 K League](/wiki/2009_K_League "2009 K League") season, Pohang once again qualified for the play-off phase of the league by finishing the regular season in second place, equal with [FC Seoul](/wiki/FC_Seoul "FC Seoul") on points, but ahead on goal difference. The Steelers had a bye to the semi-finals, but lost to Seongnam Ilhwa Chunma.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Nonetheless, their regular season placing saw them qualify for the [2010 AFC Champions League Group stage](/wiki/2010_AFC_Champions_League#Group_H "2010 AFC Champions League").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/2009_ACL_Final.jpg/250px-2009_ACL_Final.jpg)](/wiki/File:2009_ACL_Final.jpg)

Pohang defeated Al-Ittihad in Tokyo to win their third Asian championship title in 2009

Following the conclusion of the 2009 K League season, at the [2009 FIFA Club World Cup](/wiki/2009_FIFA_Club_World_Cup "2009 FIFA Club World Cup") in December, the Steelers finished in [third place](/wiki/2009_FIFA_Club_World_Cup#Match_for_third_place "2009 FIFA Club World Cup") after defeating Mexican side [Atlante](/wiki/Atlante_F.C. "Atlante F.C.") 4–3 on penalties.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Pohang saw further success on the pitch under new manager [Hwang Sun-hong](/wiki/Hwang_Sun-hong "Hwang Sun-hong") with a unique playstyle dubbed 'Steel Taka', winning the [2012](/wiki/2012_Korean_FA_Cup "2012 Korean FA Cup") and [2013](/wiki/2013_Korean_FA_Cup "2013 Korean FA Cup") editions of the Korean FA Cup, and also the [2013 K League](/wiki/2013_K_League "2013 K League") season.[[22]](#cite_note-22) By winning both competitions, the Steelers became the first club to achieve a [domestic double](/wiki/Double_(association_football) "Double (association football)") in South Korean professional football.[[23]](#cite_note-23) However, the club has not won a league title since 2013 as [Jeonbuk Hyundai Motors](/wiki/Jeonbuk_Hyundai_Motors "Jeonbuk Hyundai Motors") and later Ulsan rose to dominate the league, and went into an extended trophy drought.

In April 2019, [Kim Gi-dong](/wiki/Kim_Gi-dong "Kim Gi-dong") took over as manager. He led Pohang back to the top half of the league, and in [2020](/wiki/2020_K_League_1 "2020 K League 1"), they finished third in the league, qualifying for the AFC Champions League. The club made a strong run in the [2021 AFC Champions League](/wiki/2021_AFC_Champions_League "2021 AFC Champions League"), reaching the [final](/wiki/2021_AFC_Champions_League_final "2021 AFC Champions League final") but finishing as runners-up after losing to continental rivals [Al Hilal](/wiki/Al_Hilal_SFC "Al Hilal SFC").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

In 2023, Pohang celebrated their 50th anniversary by winning the [FA Cup](/wiki/2023_Korean_FA_Cup "2023 Korean FA Cup"), defeating Jeonbuk Hyundai in the final and securing their first FA Cup victory in ten years.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

After Kim Gi-dong left for FC Seoul in December 2023, Pohang appointed [Park Tae-ha](/wiki/Park_Tae-ha "Park Tae-ha") as the new manager. While they started the season well, their form deteriorated and the club finished sixth in the league. However, they [won the rebranded Korea Cup](/wiki/2024_Korean_FA_Cup "2024 Korean FA Cup") in 2024, defeating rivals Ulsan HD 3–1 in extra time in the final for a second consecutive cup victory.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pohang_Steelers&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Stadium")]

Main article: [Pohang Steel Yard](/wiki/Pohang_Steel_Yard "Pohang Steel Yard")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Pohang080413_6.JPG/250px-Pohang080413_6.JPG)](/wiki/File:Pohang080413_6.JPG)

Fans at the [Pohang Steel Yard](/wiki/Pohang_Steel_Yard "Pohang Steel Yard"), the first [football-specific stadium](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium") in South Korea

The Steelers' home is the [Pohang Steel Yard](/wiki/Pohang_Steel_Yard "Pohang Steel Yard"), completed in 1990 as South Korea's first [football-specific stadium](/wiki/Soccer-specific_stadium "Soccer-specific stadium").[[27]](#cite_note-27) The stadium opened in November 1990 with a match between Pohang and [Korea University](/wiki/Korea_University "Korea University").[[28]](#cite_note-28) It is located in Pohang's industrial area, close to the POSCO steelworks and next to its head office.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

The team trains at the Songna Clubhouse, located in Songna-myeon in Pohang's [North District](/wiki/Buk_District,_Pohang "Buk District, Pohang"). It was completed in 2001 at the cost of approximately 8 billion won, making the Steelers the first club in the South Korean professional football league to have their own clubhouse.[[30]](#cite_note-30) In 2019, the club opened the Steelers Football Performance Center at a cost of 2 billion won to improve players' fitness and prevent injuries.[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Club culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pohang_Steelers&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Club culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Pohang_Steelyard_safe_standing_section_N_fans.jpg/250px-Pohang_Steelyard_safe_standing_section_N_fans.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pohang_Steelyard_safe_standing_section_N_fans.jpg)

Pohang supporters in the safe standing section at the Steel Yard during an [AFC Champions League Elite](/wiki/AFC_Champions_League_Elite "AFC Champions League Elite") match in 2024

### Supporters

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pohang_Steelers&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Supporters")]

The Steelers have a number of supporters groups, including Ultras Levante, Steel Warriors, Torcida, and Marines.[[32]](#cite_note-32)[[33]](#cite_note-33) Members of supporters groups participated in the recording of the official club song "승리를 위하여 달려가자 — We are Steelers" (*Let's run for victory — We are Steelers*), written to commemorate the Steelers' 50th anniversary in 2023.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Steelers fans have friendly relations with the supporters of [Suwon Samsung Bluewings](/wiki/Suwon_Samsung_Bluewings "Suwon Samsung Bluewings") in a friendship dubbed the Supo Alliance (수포동맹). Suwon's [Frente Tricolor](/wiki/Frente_Tricolor "Frente Tricolor") supporters group raised money for the city of Pohang in the aftermath of [Typhoon Hinnamnor](/wiki/Typhoon_Hinnamnor "Typhoon Hinnamnor").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

Pohang fans made headlines in 2025 when the supporters group Ultras Levante reposted social media posts uploaded by members featuring remarks considered derogatory toward the [Jeolla](/wiki/Jeolla_Province "Jeolla Province") region, following a league match against [Gwangju FC](/wiki/Gwangju_FC "Gwangju FC").[[36]](#cite_note-36) Ultras Levante issued an apology for the behaviour of its members.[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37) Steelers chairman Lee Jong-ha initially dismissed the incident as an innocent mistake, suggesting the fans in question were too young to have intended any offence, causing further anger.[[37]](#cite_note-:1-37) Gwangju FC contacted the club directly demanding formal disciplinary action over the incident. Several days after the initial controversy, the Steelers issued an official apology and banned two fans from Pohang's home match against Gwangju later in the season.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### ROK Marines Corps

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pohang_Steelers&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: ROK Marines Corps")]

The Steelers have links with the [ROK Marine Corps](/wiki/ROK_Marine_Corps "ROK Marine Corps"), the first division of which is based in Pohang.[[39]](#cite_note-39) Marines regularly attend matches at the Steelyard and are known for singing and cheering enthusiastically.[[40]](#cite_note-40) The club has hosted special matchday events to commemorate Marines Day, and in 2019 released a special kit to celebrate the 70th anniversary of the Marine Corps.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43)

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pohang_Steelers&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Pohang_fans_in_away_end_at_Ulsan_World_Cup_stadium.jpg/250px-Pohang_fans_in_away_end_at_Ulsan_World_Cup_stadium.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pohang_fans_in_away_end_at_Ulsan_World_Cup_stadium.jpg)

Pohang away supporters at the [Ulsan Munsu Football Stadium](/wiki/Ulsan_Munsu_Football_Stadium "Ulsan Munsu Football Stadium") for the Donghaean Derby

The club's arch-rival is [Ulsan HD](/wiki/Ulsan_HD_FC "Ulsan HD FC"), in a match dubbed the [Donghaean derby](/wiki/Donghaean_derby "Donghaean derby").[[44]](#cite_note-44) The geographically close cities of Pohang and [Ulsan](/wiki/Ulsan "Ulsan") are two of the largest industrial cities in South Korea, with Pohang being home to POSCO, one of the world's largest steelmakers and Ulsan being home to [HD Hyundai](/wiki/HD_Hyundai "HD Hyundai"), the world's largest shipbuilding company. One of the most memorable matches between the two sides was played in the final round of the [2013 K League Classic](/wiki/2013_K_League_Classic "2013 K League Classic") season. Ulsan were top of the table coming into the final round, and Pohang needed to defeat Ulsan away from home to win the title. [Kim Won-il](/wiki/Kim_Won-il_(footballer) "Kim Won-il (footballer)") scored the winning goal for Pohang late in injury time for the club's fifth K League title.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Pohang also triumphed over Ulsan in the [2024 Korea Cup](/wiki/2024_Korean_FA_Cup "2024 Korean FA Cup") final to secure their record sixth cup title.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

Another rivalry is shared with [Jeonnam Dragons](/wiki/Jeonnam_Dragons "Jeonnam Dragons"), another club owned by POSCO and located in [Gwangyang](/wiki/Gwangyang "Gwangyang"), home to POSCO's largest steelworks that are even larger than the ones in Pohang. The two met in the final of the [2007 Korean FA Cup](/wiki/2007_Korean_FA_Cup "2007 Korean FA Cup"), with Pohang losing both legs of the tie.

## Kits

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Pohang_Steelers&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Kits")]

The club's original kit was orange, representing the colour of hot metal during the steelmaking process.[[47]](#cite_note-47) From 1984, red and black were established as the team's colours.[[48]](#cite_note-48) Pohang's home kit has usually featured red and black hoops, although the team wore cyan blue at home between 1996 and 1998.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

In recent years, the Steelers have released several special kits commemorating the club's history and culture. The 2018 home and away kits were released under the concept 'Back to the Original', featuring elements of designs from years gone by.[[50]](#cite_note-50) In 2019, a special kit was released to mark the 70th anniversary of the ROK Marine Corps.[[51]](#cite_note-51) The following year, the club unveiled another retro kit design, this time celebrating the 30th anniversary of the Steelers' home ground, the Steelyard.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53) In 2023, the club marked their 50th anniversary with an orange kit, bringing back the signature colour of the original POSCO FC.[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55)
